FLAG FOR OWNER: the registry codes the place as Marulanda (DANE 17446) and the date as 1963-05-05, while every press source places it in vereda La Italia, municipio Victoria, on 1963-08-05 — likely a coding/date-transposition artifact for the same event; verify before display.

I found zero primary (CNMH/Comisión de la Verdad/JEP/Rutas del Conflicto/Verdad Abierta/court) or peer-reviewed scholarship sources describing this exact event, so the >=3 primary/scholarship floor is not met; not forcing a draft. The >=3 primary/scholarship floor is therefore not verifiably met, so no draft. Reported toll (~29 total) differs from the registry's Total_Victimas_Caso of 33 — present 33 as the registry figure if drafted later.

The 3-source primary/scholarship floor is not met, so no draft.

No primary or scholarly source independently corroborates this specific framing (Air Force action, 29 victims); all available coverage attributes the attack to FARC and reports 19–25 military deaths. Extensive searching across CNMH microsites, Justice and Peace court records (BCB/Frente Sur Andaquíes proceedings), Comisión de la Verdad, Rutas del Conflicto, open Colombian data portals, academic PDFs on paramilitary activity in Caquetá, and general press archives found no published source that unambiguously identifies this specific kidnapping event (29 victims, San José del Fragua, 2002-05-28, alleged paramilitary perpetrator). Two contemporaneous archival documents in the Univalle Digital Library confirm a massacre in Rovira, Tolima in 1959 attributed to bandoleros (including the group of Teófilo Rojas Varón, alias 'Chispas'), and two regional press sources (El Nuevo Día, Qhubo Ibagué) corroborate a specific attack on the vereda La Palmita on May 9, 1959 killing 25 campesinos. There is also a material discrepancy: press sources cite May 9, 1959 and 25 victims, while the CNMH registry records May 19, 1959 and 28 victims, possibly reflecting a multi-day event or a data-entry difference.

The only plausibly related press source found is an El Tiempo article published February 22, 2002, reporting combat in El Placer/El Queremal, Dagua (FARC Frente 30 vs. paramilitaries, ~45 dead: 27 FARC + 18 paramilitary) beginning around February 20 — date and location are consistent but the victim count (45 vs. 28 coded) and the inter-armed-group nature of the clash leave the match uncertain. No primary sources (CNMH microsite, JEP, Comisión de la Verdad, Rutas del Conflicto, Fiscalía, court rulings) were found that unambiguously identify IdCaso 17340 with 28 victims on 2002-02-20 in Dagua. The ≥3 primary/scholarship threshold is not met.

Exhaustive search across Colombian conflict archives (CINEP/Noche y Niebla, Rutas del Conflicto, Comisión de la Verdad, CIDH, HRW, Amnesty International, UCDP, El Tiempo archivo, Semana, El Espectador, Wikipedia chronologies) found no published source that unambiguously describes an armed action (acción bélica) in Dagua, Valle del Cauca on or around September 7, 1999 with 28 victims attributed to the Ejército Nacional. Extensive searching in Spanish and English across CNMH Observatory pages, Rutas del Conflicto, JEP case documentation, Human Rights Watch, Amnesty International, Wikipedia chronologies, and press archives found no published source that unambiguously describes a November 10, 1992 combat action (acción bélica) in Valle del Guamuez, Putumayo with 28 victims attributed to the Ejército Nacional. The only well-documented Putumayo event close in date is the Toma de Churuyaco (November 7, 1992, Orito municipality, perpetrator FARC, 26 police killed), which is a different municipality, date, and perpetrator. The JEP Caso 03 Putumayo sub-case involves the Batallón de Infantería Héroes del Valle del Guamuez, but that unit operated 1998–2012, postdating this record by six years. No primary, scholarship, or tertiary source independently corroborating this specific event was found.

Extensive searching found no source that unambiguously confirms a forced disappearance event in Barrancabermeja, Santander on May 6, 1998 with 28 victims. The only well-documented mass forced disappearance event in that municipality and month is the "Masacre del 16 de mayo de 1998" (AUSAC paramilitaries, 7 killed + 25 disappeared = 32 victims), which is thoroughly documented by Rutas del Conflicto, CNMH, CEJIL, CAJAR, Amnesty International (AMR23/037/1998), the IACHR (Case 12.434), and the Inter-American Court (Cañas Cano y otros vs. Colombia). The three-variable mismatch — date (May 6 vs. May 16), victim count (28 vs. 32), and attributed perpetrator (GRUPO ARMADO NO IDENTIFICADO vs. AUSAC paramilitaries) — prevents safe identification of IdCaso 236683 with that event. It may represent a separate, less-documented incident or a different disaggregation within the same operation; no confirming primary or secondary source was found for the specific coded record.

Exhaustive web searches in Spanish and English across CNMH portals, Rutas del Conflicto, Wikipedia violence annexes, Banco de la República, DeJusticia, and Tolima-specific academic and historical sources yielded no published source that unambiguously identifies this specific event — an atentado terrorista in Alvarado, Tolima on 4 May 1958 with 27 victims. The event dates to the final days of La Violencia, a period with very sparse event-level documentation outside the CNMH's own OMC database; the minimum threshold of 3 primary or scholarship sources cannot be met.

The event is unambiguously identified as the "Masacre de La Italia" perpetrated by José William Aranguren alias Desquite on August 5, 1963, in vereda La Italia, Victoria, Caldas — place, date, and bandolero perpetrator all match the coded record. However, all corroborating sources found are tertiary press or journalism; no primary sources (CNMH report URL, Rutas del Conflicto, Verdad Abierta, JEP, court ruling) or peer-reviewed scholarship covering this specific event were located, so the ≥3 primary/scholarship threshold is not met.

The event corresponds to the FARC attack on the anti-narcotics police base in Miraflores, Guaviare on August 6-7, 1995, during which civilians, police, and FARC combatants died, with the Colombian state subsequently held administratively responsible for civilian casualties by the Consejo de Estado (case 1996-05750). Only one primary source (the Consejo de Estado ruling, which could not be fully read as the PDF is binary-encoded) was identified; the two remaining confirming sources are tertiary. The 3-primary/scholarship threshold is not met, so no narrative has been drafted.

The event is unambiguously identified across multiple sources as the Emboscada de la Quebrada Riecito (Puerto Rico, Caquetá, mid-June 1987): FARC Frentes 14 and 15 ambushed two Cazadores Battalion trucks on the Puerto Rico–San Vicente del Caguán road, killing 26–27 soldiers. However, all confirming sources are tertiary (press, Wikipedia, NGO archive); no primary institutional sources (CNMH report, JEP, Comisión de la Verdad, Rutas del Conflicto, Verdad Abierta, or court ruling) were found that independently document this specific event, so the 3-primary/scholarship threshold is not met. Note also that all sources attribute this action to FARC-EP, while the CNMH coded perpetrator is "AGENTE DEL ESTADO / EJÉRCITO NACIONAL" — in bélica coding this likely identifies the state combatant party (the Ejército column) rather than the attacking actor.

The event is identifiable: on November 17, 1998, FARC's Bloque José María Córdoba (Fronts 5, 34, and 57, approximately 400 combatants) attacked paramilitary positions in the Pavarandó corregimiento of Mutatá, Antioquia, in what CNMH classifies as an Acción Bélica. Two El Tiempo archive articles (MAM-810614 and MAM-816715) confirm the date, location, and parties, but they are tertiary press sources. No primary sources (CNMH monograph, JEP Caso 04 ruling, Comisión de la Verdad document, Verdad Abierta, Fiscalía, or court ruling) were found that specifically document this IdCaso; the ≥3 primary/scholarship threshold is not met.

Extensive searches in Spanish and English across Rutas del Conflicto, Verdad Abierta, CNMH online resources, Wikipedia conflict chronologies, El Tiempo archives, UN OHCHR Colombia reports, CIDH annual reports, and general news databases returned no published source that unambiguously describes an ACCIÓN BÉLICA in Ituango, Antioquia on or around January 25, 2005 with 27 victims attributed to guerrilla forces. The event is unambiguously identified as Operación Fortaleza II (September 19, 2010, San Miguel, Putumayo, Fuerza Aérea + FARC Frente 48, 27 victims) across five independent press sources that match all coded-record fields. The event is consistently documented across 5 sources (2 primary, 3 tertiary press), all converging on place (El Valle, Bahía Solano, Chocó), date (August 20, 2002), victim count (27), and perpetrator (ELN). The event is the "Toma del Cerro Girasoles" (January 8-9, 1991, Mesetas/La Macarena, Meta): FARC-EP Bloque Oriental, Front 26, commanded by Mono Jojoy, Martín Sombra, and El Cura Camilo, attacked a platoon from Battalion 21 Vargas, killing 2 soldiers and kidnapping 16-17 (CNMH records 17; most press sources corroborate 17, Wikipedia says 16). Two court convictions specifically for this attack are documented in press: Martín Sombra (24 years 7 months, Juzgado Penal del Circuito Especializado de Villavicencio, October 2008) and El Cura Camilo (10 years). However, the court ruling documents are not accessible online; the only identified scholarly source (Hernández Torres, Pontificia Universidad Javeriana, 2013, confirmed to begin with the Girasoles attack) returned HTTP 403; and no CNMH/JEP/Verdad Abierta/Rutas del Conflicto page for this specific event was found accessible. Coverage is rich in press (tertiary) but falls below the required three primary/scholarship sources.

Six targeted searches found no source that specifically identifies this event — a 17-victim forced disappearance in Santa Marta on 2001-08-05 attributed to the Autodefensas de Hernán Giraldo. The 2019 Justice and Peace sentence (Tribunal Superior de Barranquilla) covers 755 forced disappearance cases in Santa Marta 1996–2004 attributed to Giraldo Serna, but no named incident matching this date and scale could be isolated from any primary, scholarship, or tertiary source.

Rutas del Conflicto (CNMH) documents this event in full detail: on June 10, 2001, Bloque Sur Putumayo paramilitaries entered Puerto Umbría corregimiento in Villagarzón, ordered a 72-hour evacuation, then called 17 residents by name, took them to La Cristalina vereda, killed them, and dumped the bodies in the Putumayo River (16 unidentified); named victim Melba Erazo, former concejal, and two minors are confirmed. Only 1 verified primary source was located; Verdad Abierta and a CNMH Putumayo report are cited as underlying sources by Rutas del Conflicto but no working URLs for those records were found, leaving the ≥3 primary/scholarship threshold unmet.

Six targeted searches in Spanish and English across CNMH, Rutas del Conflicto, Verdad Abierta, Comisión de la Verdad, Justicia y Paz, HRW, and Amnesty International sources returned no source that unambiguously describes this specific event — the forced disappearance of 17 persons in Amalfi, Antioquia on June 4, 1997 attributed to Autodefensas Campesinas del Magdalena Medio. Abundant general context about ACMM operations and forced disappearances in the Magdalena Medio/northeast Antioquia region exists, but no indexed public source isolates this date, place, and victim count.

The car bomb at Talleres Fullton (Calle 28, Barrio Palmira, Barrancabermeja) on February 10, 1993 is corroborated by three contemporaneous El Tiempo archive reports and a 2015 Vanguardia retrospective, all tertiary press. No primary or scholarship sources were located — Rutas del Conflicto, Verdad Abierta, Comisión de la Verdad, and CNMH published reports do not cover this event; a Consejo de Estado PDF (case 25000-23-26-000-1995-00595-01/18860) surfaced in searches but was unreadable as compressed binary, preventing verification of its scope. The case does not meet the three-primary-source threshold.

The event is corroborated in two Wikipedia timeline articles, which record that on 26 September 1963 troops of the Batallón Caicedo killed 16 members of communist self-defense forces in the Cañón de la Troja, Natagaima (Tolima), including commanders Plácido Aragón "capitán Tolima" and Gratiniano Rocha "Avenegra". Both Wikipedia entries carry "[cita requerida]" markers (no underlying citations provided). No primary sources (CNMH reports, JEP filings, Comisión de la Verdad, Rutas del Conflicto, Verdad Abierta, court documents) or peer-reviewed scholarship documenting this specific event were found; the >=3 primary/scholarly threshold is not met.

Four rounds of targeted web searches (Spanish and English) across CNMH, Rutas del Conflicto, academic databases, and press archives returned no published source that unambiguously identifies a masacre in Barbosa, Santander on 28 January 1964 with 16 victims attributed to bandolerismo. Rutas del Conflicto's massacre database begins in 1982; the CNMH SIEVCAC registry is the sole confirming record found, which is insufficient to draft a narrative.

Six targeted searches and multiple document fetches found no published source that unambiguously describes a massacre in Cimitarra, Santander on 1983-08-08 with 16 victims. The nearest documented events are the Vuelta Acuña massacre (Cimitarra, January 1984, ~10-20 victims, MAS + XIV Brigade) and a 1987 Cimitarra massacre (14 victims) — neither matches on date. The event likely predates the documentation window of most accessible Colombian conflict archives, which begin systematic tracking of paramilitary massacres from 1984-1985 onward.

Six targeted web searches in Spanish and English (Rutas del Conflicto, CNMH, Comisión de la Verdad, Wikipedia, HRW, press archives) returned no published source that unambiguously describes a February 1968 massacre in San Alberto, Cesar with ~16 victims attributed to the Colombian Army. The event predates the online coverage window of all major conflict-documentation portals (Rutas del Conflicto starts 1982; Comisión de la Verdad focuses on 1985–2016); no primary, scholarship, or tertiary source was found to meet the corroboration threshold.

Multiple targeted web searches in Spanish and English (place, date, victim count, perpetrator type, Rutas del Conflicto site search, CNMH tag pages) returned no sources that unambiguously describe a massacre in Pital, Huila on or around April 25, 1961 with ~16 victims. The bandolerismo period (late 1950s–mid 1960s) in Huila is almost entirely absent from digitally accessible primary or scholarly sources; no corroborating source meeting the ≥3 primary/scholarship threshold was found.

Check 2 fails — 'Tribunal de Justicia y Paz de Medellín' as the specific venue where witnesses implicated López Gómez is not confirmed by any fetched source (Rutas del Conflicto, Wikipedia, El Colombiano, El Espectador all mention López Gómez and/or the Ralito Pact conviction, but none name this specific tribunal or testimony); this is a positive factual claim in the narrative without source support. | Check 2 fails — bus interception at 'corregimiento de Popayán (San Pedro de Urabá, Antioquia)' is not confirmed by any fetched source; El Espectador confirms a bus on the Montería–Arboletes route was commandeered but does not name Popayán as the interception stop; El Colombiano confirms bus interception but also gives no specific stop. | Check 4 borderline fails — of the three sources labeled 'primary' (Rutas del Conflicto, Unidad para las Víctimas, Alcaldía de Canalete), only Rutas returned usable substantive content; both the Unidad para las Víctimas and Alcaldía pages rendered as essentially empty (JS-heavy shells), so they cannot be verified as live content-bearing sources supporting the narrative's specific claims.

Multiple targeted searches across Rutas del Conflicto, CNMH microsites, Wikipedia's FARC massacres annex, and HRW archives found no published source independently documenting a FARC massacre in Tibú on July 17, 2001 with 16 victims. The only 2001 FARC events in this municipio with published corroboration are the La Gabarra massacres of September 6 and 10, 2001; this event appears in the CNMH SIEVCAC registry but lacks independent published coverage sufficient to draft a narrative.

Rutas del Conflicto (primary) provides a dedicated account confirming January 17, 2000, 16 victims across four veredas of Yarumal (La Quiebra, El Llano, La Rivera, Ochalí), attributed to Bloque Mineros/AUC — fully consistent with the coded record. Four tertiary sources independently corroborate the event with the same victim count and AUC attribution, though Hacemos Memoria (UdeA) gives the date as January 6 rather than January 17. No additional primary or peer-reviewed sources explicitly documenting this specific event were located, leaving only one primary source against the required three-source floor.

Eight targeted web searches in Spanish and English (varying perpetrator, date, location, victim count) returned no specific published coverage of this event. No press archive, human rights report, PAICMA/Acción Contra Minas incident record, Rutas del Conflicto entry, or scholarly source independently confirms this MINAS event in San José del Guaviare on 2007-02-20 with 16 victims attributed to the Ejército Nacional. The event appears only in the CNMH SIEVCAC registry, which is the coded record itself rather than an independently verifiable corroborating source.

Three rounds of targeted web searches in Spanish and English — covering the Colombian Air Force (FAC) news archive, El Tiempo archive, Wikipedia armed-conflict chronologies (2000–2019), Rutas del Conflicto, Semana Cronología, CINEP, and El Universal — returned no published source that unambiguously describes this specific acción bélica in Puerto Rico, Meta on or around July 23, 2009 with 16 victims attributed to the Fuerza Aérea. Combat engagements of this type (Air Force strike on guerrilla combatants) were routinely absorbed into aggregate press tallies and did not receive standalone indexed coverage unless they were named operations; this event has no such named-operation footprint in publicly accessible sources.

Nine targeted searches across CNMH efemerides, Rutas del Conflicto, Verdad Abierta, CINEP Noche y Niebla no. 23 (Jan–Mar 2002), El Tiempo archive, Semana, Wikipedia conflict chronologies, and HRW 2003 annual report found no authoritative published source that specifically documents this acción bélica in La Sierra, Cauca on 2002-03-26. CINEP records confirm La Sierra (Macizo Colombiano) was an active FARC/Army combat zone in this period, but no primary or scholarly source narrates this specific event or its 16 victims.

The Wikipedia article on Colombia's 2002-2010 armed conflict confirms a combat on December 23, 2006 in the La Julia inspection of La Uribe (Meta) between FARC-EP and the Ejército Nacional, listing 54 total deaths. A Colombian presidential press-office URL from December 24, 2006 (historico.presidencia.gov.co) was indexed under the title "Militares asesinados en 'La Julia' impidieron ataque a esa población" but the server was unreachable. No primary source (CNMH report, JEP resolution, Fiscalía indictment, Comisión de la Verdad entry, Rutas del Conflicto, Verdad Abierta, or court ruling) was found that specifically identifies the 16 civilian victims recorded in the CNMH as an Acción Bélica attributed to the Ejército Nacional. Threshold of 3 primary/scholarship sources not met.

Six targeted searches across CINEP Noche y Niebla (issues 12 and 14, 1999), El Tiempo archive, Semana conflict chronology, Rutas del Conflicto, and general web sources found no published source independently documenting this specific June 28, 1999 acción bélica in Iquira, Huila with 16 victims attributed to the Ejército Nacional. The event record exists only in the CNMH SIEVCAC registry; no corroborating primary, scholarly, or tertiary source was located.

Eight targeted searches in Spanish and English across Rutas del Conflicto, JEP Caso 03 Antioquia subcase, CCEEU eastern Antioquia extrajudicial killings report, CNMH Observatorio, and general press turned up no source that unambiguously identifies this specific acción bélica (Sonsón, April 20 2003, 16 victims, Ejército Nacional). The only Sonsón 2003 event with authoritative coverage is a FARC massacre in La Danta on February 22, 2003 (5 victims, distinct event). JEP Caso 03 covers Sonsón in its Oriente Antioqueño subcase but does not surface this specific April 2003 incident at the detail level accessible via web sources.

Approximately 15 targeted searches across CINEP Noche y Niebla (issue 13, Jul-Sep 1999), CIDH annual reports, Rutas del Conflicto, El Tiempo archive, HRW, CNMH Observatorio, and Semana's violence chronology found no source that unambiguously identifies this specific acción bélica in Turbo, Antioquia on 1999-08-01. Combat engagements attributed to the Ejército Nacional (as opposed to civilian massacres) generate substantially less independent press and NGO documentation in publicly indexed sources for this period; the event may exist only in military records and the CNMH SIEVCAC registry itself. No draft produced.

The April 20, 2006 armed confrontation at vereda Astilleros, Hacarí (Norte de Santander) is the most significant single loss of DAS personnel in the agency's history: an EPL (Frente Libardo Mora Toro) ambush of a joint DAS-Army convoy mounted to capture Víctor Ramón Navarro alias Megateo, triggered by advance intelligence leaked by a corrupt DAS agent. The event is thoroughly confirmed across seven independent press sources and one peer-reviewed journal article (Reflexión Política/Redalyc), which puts the toll at 16 (10 DAS + 6 military) — matching the CNMH figure exactly; press sources report 17. JEP proceedings (Sala de Definición de Situaciones Jurídicas, Catatumbo subsection) adjudicated the case of convicted informant Carlos Alberto Suárez Reyes in 2024 and explicitly reference this event, constituting a primary JEP record, but the actual JEP resolution documents were inaccessible during this search. With only one confirmed scholarship source and no directly verified primary document, the record falls short of the ≥3 primary/scholarship floor required for drafted status.

Three rounds of targeted searches (Spanish and English) across Rutas del Conflicto, CNMH tag archives, CRIC, and general press returned no authoritative secondary source describing a specific acción bélica on 12 May 1986 in Jambaló, Cauca attributed to the Ejército Nacional with 16 victims. The event type (combat action, not massacre) and the early date (1986) place it in a period and category that are systematically under-documented in published memorial and human-rights literature; the minimum of 3 primary/scholarship sources cannot be met.

Seven targeted searches across CIDH reports, CNMH publications, HRW/Amnesty documents, JEP records, Comisión de la Verdad, Rutas del Conflicto, and Wikipedia yielded no source that specifically identifies this acción bélica in San Carlos, Antioquia on 22 September 1999 attributed to the Ejército Nacional. San Carlos has extensive conflict documentation but it centers on paramilitary masacres from the same period; this army combat action with 16 victims does not appear in any accessible authoritative publication.

Two sources confirm a major armed engagement in Santa María, Huila on February 24, 2004 — the FARC Frente Joselo Lozada attacked the Army's Batallón de Artillería Tenerife at Cerro San Sebastián, killing 12 soldiers and wounding several more — but both sources attribute perpetration to FARC, not the Ejército Nacional as coded in IdCaso 22498. No source was found confirming an Army-initiated action with 16 victims on that date, and the minimum of 3 primary/scholarship sources confirming the CNMH-coded event is not met.

Four targeted web searches in Spanish and English (place, date, perpetrator, victim count, IdCaso) found no authoritative published source that unambiguously documents an acción bélica on November 16, 1982 in Valparaíso, Caquetá attributed to the Ejército Nacional with 16 victims. General histories of the armed conflict in Caquetá around 1982 exist but do not mention this specific engagement.

The event is identifiable as the "Emboscada de La Perdiz" (March 3, 1967, road from Santana Ramos to Algeciras, Huila): a FARC unit of 40-50 combatants under Januario Valero ambushed a 22-soldier patrol from Batallón de Infantería N°9 Boyacá, killing 16 (2 sub-officers + 14 soldiers). Date, location, and victim count match exactly, but all corroborating sources are tertiary (Wikipedia citing 1967 El Tiempo newspaper archives); no primary or scholarship sources (CNMH thematic reports, Comisión de la Verdad, JEP, Rutas del Conflicto, peer-reviewed scholarship) were found. Additionally, the CNMH-coded perpetrator "AGENTE DEL ESTADO / EJÉRCITO NACIONAL" contradicts all independent sources, which attribute the attack to FARC — this discrepancy likely reflects a CNMH coding convention for acciones bélicas where the state party is always listed, but cannot be resolved without access to the original case file.

Twelve targeted searches across CNMH Observatory, Rutas del Conflicto, JEP Caso 03 records, CINEP Noche y Niebla (issues 25 and 26 covering 2002), Observatorio de DDHH del Gobierno, FIP conflict dynamics reports, and Colombian news archives (El Tiempo, El Espectador) returned no source that unambiguously identifies this specific acción bélica in Planadas, Tolima on 5 September 2002. Combat events in this FARC-stronghold municipality during 2002 are referenced only in aggregate statistics; individual acción bélica incidents from that zone and date are not individually documented in any accessible primary or scholarship source.

The combat action in Albán, Nariño on January 13–14, 2002 is confirmed: FARC (~200 combatants) attacked the police station, killing 9 named officers and 1 civilian (10 deaths per El Tiempo; CNMH Memorias Visuales confirms the date). However, CNMH IdCaso 17084 codes AGENTE DEL ESTADO / POLICÍA NACIONAL as perpetrator with 16 victims, likely recording guerrilla combatants killed by police during the same engagement — a sub-event for which no authoritative source documenting those 16 deaths was found. Only 1 primary source identified; the ≥3 threshold was not met.

Four rounds of targeted searches in Spanish and English (CNMH, Rutas del Conflicto, HRW, FAC/JEP reports, regional Boyacá conflict literature, El Tiempo archives, press) turned up no source that unambiguously describes an Air Force combat action in Chita, Boyacá on or around December 17, 2002 with 16 victims. The event is likely a low-profile armed engagement that was not independently documented in accessible public sources.

Six rounds of targeted searches in Spanish and English — across CNMH documents, Amnesty International's Arauca report, Semana's conflict chronology, Rutas del Conflicto, Wikipedia's armed-conflict chronology, and academic sources — returned no published source that unambiguously describes this specific acción bélica (Tame, Arauca, 22 August 1984, 16 victims, AGENTE DEL ESTADO / POLICÍA NACIONAL). General contextual documentation of ELN growth in Arauca during 1984 exists but does not identify this particular event; early-1980s individual incidents in the department are largely undocumented in available open sources.
